casetaking.sig: Document onset (when did it start), duration, character, severity (0-10 scale), modifying factors (what makes it better or worse), and associated symptoms. In naturopathic assessment, also document: time of day of worst symptoms (circadian), relationship to meals, emotional or stress triggers, and seasonal pattern.
casetaking.cond: Key questions: Did anything change 3 to 6 months before onset? (Hering's Law — suppression often precedes new pathology). Which direction are symptoms moving — centripetal (inward, worsening) or centrifugal (outward, healing)?
casetaking.inv: Timeline: construct a life events timeline from birth to present — significant illnesses, surgeries, vaccinations, major life stressors, dietary shifts, relationship changes

casetaking.sig: 24-hour dietary recall (at least 3 days). Document: meal timing (circadian alignment), food preparation methods, water intake (quality and quantity), stimulant use (caffeine, alcohol, tobacco), food intolerances and elimination history.
casetaking.cond: Assess: Omega-3 to omega-6 ratio, refined carbohydrate load, protein adequacy, fibre (soluble vs insoluble), phytonutrient diversity, fermented food intake, processed food percentage.
casetaking.inv: Nutritional markers to consider: serum B12, folate, vitamin D (25-OH), ferritin, zinc, magnesium (RBC magnesium preferred), omega-3 index, fasting glucose, HbA1c

casetaking.sig: Document: sleep onset time, wake time, total hours, sleep quality (refreshed or unrefreshed on waking), dream recall, night sweats, nocturia (how many times), snoring or witnessed apnoea, position of sleep.
casetaking.cond: Naturopathic significance: Unrefreshed sleep despite adequate hours = adrenal fatigue or sleep-disordered breathing. Early morning waking (3 to 4 AM) = Liver Qi stagnation or cortisol dysregulation. Difficulty falling asleep = sympathetic dominance (excess cortisol or adrenaline at night).
casetaking.inv: Pittsburgh Sleep Quality Index (PSQI) score, cortisol diurnal curve, 8 AM and 4 PM and midnight cortisol, DHEA-S, polysomnography if apnoea suspected

casetaking.sig: Frequency (ideal: once or twice per day), consistency (Bristol Stool Chart Type 3 to 4 is optimal), colour (bile-stained golden-brown = good hepatobiliary function), odour, presence of undigested food, mucus, or blood. Also assess: urgency, straining, incomplete evacuation.
casetaking.cond: Naturopathic significance: Constipation (Type 1 to 2) = magnesium deficiency, hypothyroid, dysbiosis, dehydration. Loose stools (Type 6 to 7) = SIBO, gut hyperpermeability, food intolerance, bile acid malabsorption. Pale stools = biliary obstruction or fat malabsorption.
casetaking.inv: Stool analysis (PCR/culture), SIBO lactulose breath test, calprotectin, faecal elastase, serum magnesium, TSH

casetaking.sig: Assess: current life stressors (work, relationship, financial, existential), perceived stress score, coping mechanisms, trauma history (ACE — Adverse Childhood Events — score), and social support network. Assess emotional patterns: suppression, perfectionism, people-pleasing, grief.
casetaking.cond: Naturopathic significance: chronic psychological stress perpetuates HPA axis dysregulation, suppresses immune surveillance, elevates inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, TNF-alpha), disrupts gut motility, and impairs nutrient absorption.
casetaking.inv: Perceived Stress Scale (PSS-10), PHQ-9 (depression screening), GAD-7 (anxiety screening), diurnal cortisol curve, DHEA-S, salivary secretory IgA

casetaking.sig: Assess: occupational exposures (chemicals, heavy metals, solvents, electromagnetic fields), home environment (mould, cleaning products, plastics, air quality), personal care product load (parabens, phthalates), water source quality, medication burden (total drug and supplement load).
casetaking.cond: Key naturopathic concept: Toxic load (toxins in) minus detoxification capacity (toxins out) determines tissue burden. Symptoms of high toxic load: fatigue, brain fog, chemical sensitivities, multiple unexplained symptoms, poor liver function markers.
casetaking.inv: Heavy metals panel (urine or hair), organochlorine pesticides, phthalates and bisphenol A, LFT (GGT is a sensitive liver toxic stress marker), RBC glutathione

casetaking.sig: Document: all previous diagnoses, surgeries, hospitalisations. Crucially for naturopathic assessment — note history of suppressive treatments (steroids, antibiotics, immunosuppressants) as these may have driven pathology inward (Hering's Law reversal). Document all previous naturopathic, Ayurvedic, homeopathic treatments and response.
casetaking.cond: Key naturopathic question: Were there acute disease episodes that were suppressed rather than resolved (frequent antibiotic courses for recurrent otitis media, repeated steroid courses for eczema, etc.)? These suppression patterns often predict current deeper pathology.
casetaking.inv: Request complete previous investigation records. Construct a suppression-to-pathology timeline to identify cause and effect patterns.

vitalforce.sig: The Vital Force is the inherent self-regulating, self-healing intelligence of the organism — Hippocrates' Physis, Hahnemann's Lebenskraft, Traditional Chinese Medicine's Qi, Ayurveda's Prana. Clinically assessed through: response to treatment, healing speed, and the direction of symptom movement.
vitalforce.cond: Strong Vital Force signs: rapid healing, good energy, adequate sleep, regular elimination, emotional resilience, strong disease reactions (acute febrile response). Weak Vital Force: slow healing, chronic fatigue, poor immune response, flat affect, irregular elimination, tendency to chronicity.
vitalforce.inv: No single test — clinical gestalt assessment. Supportive markers: secretory IgA (mucosal immunity), NK cell activity, diurnal cortisol, mitochondrial function (VO2 max, organic acids test), omega-3 index

vitalforce.sig: Constantine Hering (1800-1880) observed that healing proceeds: (1) from above downward, (2) from within outward (internal to external — deeper to surface), (3) from more vital to less vital organs, (4) in reverse order of appearance (last symptoms disappear first).
vitalforce.cond: Clinical application: skin rashes appearing during treatment of internal disease may represent outward movement (centrifugal) and a healing reaction — do not suppress. New skin eruption + improvement in asthma = positive Hering's direction. New joint pain + improvement in heart condition = positive direction.
vitalforce.inv: Track symptom changes on a body map timeline. Document: what improved first? What appeared new? Are symptoms moving from deep (visceral, CNS) to surface (skin, joints)? Suppression reverses this direction.

vitalforce.sig: Healing Crisis (Herxheimer reaction or Jarisch-Herxheimer): temporary intensification of symptoms during genuine healing. FEATURES — brief duration (24 to 72 hours), accompanied by sense of underlying improvement, follows Hering's direction, patient feels 'sick but somehow better', increased elimination (sweating, loose stools, urination).
vitalforce.cond: Disease Crisis (worsening): symptoms intensify without underlying improvement. FEATURES — sustained worsening (>72 hours), patient feels globally worse, no sense of improvement, symptoms move inward (new organ involvement), energy declining. KEY DISTINCTION: healing crisis resolves spontaneously; disease crisis requires intervention.
vitalforce.inv: Monitor: temperature, energy trend (improving vs declining), elimination (increased = healing), mood (crisis = maintained or improved mood despite symptoms). If uncertain: reduce treatment intensity and observe direction of change.

vitalforce.sig: Acute inflammatory and febrile responses are vital force in action — the organism's attempt to eliminate pathogens, restore homeostasis, and strengthen immunity. Naturopathic principle: support the acute response rather than suppress it (unless dangerous).
vitalforce.cond: Fever management thresholds: below 38.5 degrees Celsius (101.3 F) in adults — support with hydrotherapy (cool compresses, wet sheet pack). 38.5 to 40 degrees Celsius — monitor closely, support elimination. Above 40 degrees Celsius (104 F), young children, immunocompromised — conventional intervention warranted. Never suppress fever in the first 24 hours unless dangerously high.
vitalforce.inv: Temperature (tympanic or rectal), CBC (WBC differential — neutrophilia = bacterial; lymphocytosis = viral), CRP, blood cultures if indicated, SpO2

vitalforce.sig: Type 1 — Morning fatigue, improves through day: adrenal fatigue pattern (cortisol blunted at morning peak). Type 2 — Afternoon energy crash (2 to 4 PM): blood sugar dysregulation, postprandial hypoglycaemia, mitochondrial inefficiency. Type 3 — Fatigue throughout day, no variation: systemic (hypothyroid, anaemia, chronic infection). Type 4 — Post-exertional fatigue (worsens after activity): mitochondrial dysfunction, myalgic encephalomyelitis / chronic fatigue syndrome.
vitalforce.cond: Type 1: diurnal cortisol, DHEA-S, blood pressure (orthostatic), sodium and potassium. Type 2: fasting glucose, insulin, HOMA-IR, C-peptide. Type 3: TSH, CBC, ferritin, B12, LFT. Type 4: VO2 max testing, 2-day CPET, mitochondrial function testing, NK cell activity.
vitalforce.inv: Diurnal cortisol curve (4-point saliva), DHEA-S, fasting insulin and glucose, TSH and FT4, CBC, ferritin, serum B12, vitamin D (25-OH), magnesium (RBC)

vitalforce.sig: Pain character in naturopathic assessment: Burning pain = heat pathology (Pitta, inflammation, nerve irritation). Aching, heavy pain = damp or cold pathology (Kapha, venous congestion, lymphatic stagnation). Sharp, migrating pain = Vata pathology (gas, nerve, spasm). Dull, fixed pain = blood stasis or structural pathology. Time pattern: morning stiffness = inflammatory arthritis. Evening pain = venous insufficiency. Night pain = bone pathology or cancer (red flag).
vitalforce.cond: Red flags requiring urgent conventional investigation: unexplained weight loss + pain, night pain disturbing sleep, pain in child under 16 with systemic features, pain associated with neurological deficit, first or worst headache of life, jaw claudication (temporal arteritis).
vitalforce.inv: VAS (Visual Analogue Scale) 0 to 10, McGill Pain Questionnaire, inflammatory markers (CRP, ESR), musculoskeletal USS or MRI if structural, nerve conduction studies if neuropathic

terrain.sig: Antoine Bechamp and Claude Bernard proposed that the internal environment (terrain) of the organism determines susceptibility to disease — contrasting with Pasteur's germ theory (external cause). Naturopathic medicine synthesises both: germs are necessary but not sufficient — terrain determines expression. A healthy terrain resists pathological microbial overgrowth.
terrain.cond: The 6 key terrain factors: (1) Acid-base balance. (2) Oxygenation and circulation. (3) Toxic load and detoxification. (4) Nutritional status and enzyme function. (5) Electromagnetic balance and nerve function. (6) Psychological and emotional state.
terrain.inv: Terrain assessment tools: urine and saliva pH (morning, first void), RBC morphology, dark-field microscopy (live blood analysis — not validated diagnostically), HRV, toxic load panel, comprehensive nutritional panel

terrain.sig: Optimal urinary pH: 6.4 to 7.0 (morning first void). Persistently acidic urine (pH below 6.0) = metabolic acidosis tendency — excess protein, processed food, stress-driven acid production, dehydration, impaired bicarbonate buffer. Persistently alkaline urine (pH above 7.5) = vegetarian diet (normal), urinary tract infection (ammonia-producing bacteria), renal tubular acidosis (paradoxical).
terrain.cond: Tissue acidosis consequences: impaired enzyme function (enzymes operate optimally at physiological pH), reduced oxygen delivery (Bohr effect), impaired mineral uptake (magnesium, calcium, potassium), promotes inflammatory microenvironment, impairs hepatic detoxification.
terrain.inv: Urine pH (litmus paper, morning first void, track over 7 days), serum bicarbonate, ABG (in severe cases), anion gap, serum lactate, urinalysis

terrain.sig: Optimal tissue oxygenation requires: (1) adequate red blood cell count and haemoglobin, (2) adequate iron and B12 and folate for erythropoiesis, (3) good microcirculation (capillary integrity), (4) diaphragmatic breathing (most people use only 30% of lung capacity), (5) physical activity to drive lymphatic and venous return.
terrain.cond: Signs of poor oxygenation terrain: pallor, cold extremities, brain fog, afternoon fatigue, poor wound healing, frequent infections, low SpO2. Naturopathic interventions: hydrotherapy (contrast showers), deep diaphragmatic breathing (pranayama), aerobic exercise, iron and B12 if deficient, Ginkgo biloba for microcirculation.
terrain.inv: SpO2 (pulse oximetry), CBC (Hb, haematocrit, RBC indices), serum ferritin, B12, folate, TIBC, spirometry, VO2 max testing

terrain.sig: Phase 1 hepatic detoxification (CYP450 enzymes) converts fat-soluble toxins to reactive intermediates. Phase 2 conjugation (glucuronidation, sulphation, methylation, glutathione conjugation) renders them water-soluble for excretion. Impaired Phase 2 relative to Phase 1 = accumulation of reactive intermediates = increased oxidative stress.
terrain.cond: Signs of impaired detoxification terrain: chemical sensitivities (perfumes, exhaust), slow medication metabolism (drug side effects at normal doses), alcohol intolerance, caffeine hypersensitivity, multiple food intolerances, persistent fatigue and brain fog despite adequate rest.
terrain.inv: LFT (GGT = most sensitive Phase 1 marker), RBC glutathione, plasma cysteine, urinary sulphate-to-creatinine ratio, heavy metals panel, organic acids test (mitochondrial function and detoxification markers), MTHFR gene testing (methylation pathway)

terrain.sign: Nutritional Terrain — Enzyme and Cofactor Status
terrain.sig: Every metabolic reaction requires enzyme cofactors — vitamins and minerals serve as essential cofactors. Subclinical deficiencies impair enzymatic function before producing classical deficiency symptoms. Magnesium is a cofactor for >300 enzymatic reactions; zinc for >200; B vitamins for energy metabolism, neurotransmitter synthesis, and methylation.
terrain.cond: Subclinical deficiency patterns: Magnesium — muscle cramps, insomnia, anxiety, constipation, hypertension. Zinc — poor wound healing, taste or smell impairment, frequent infections, white spots on nails. B6 — peripheral tingling, PMS, dream recall impaired. B12 — fatigue, neurological symptoms, macrocytosis. Vitamin D — musculoskeletal pain, fatigue, low mood, frequent infections.
terrain.inv: RBC magnesium (preferred over serum), serum zinc, serum B12, plasma B6 (PLP), vitamin D (25-OH), serum folate, RBC folate, hair mineral analysis (longitudinal mineral status)

terrain.sig: The nervous system's bioelectrical integrity determines tissue communication, hormonal signalling, and immune coordination. Vagal tone (parasympathetic dominance) is essential for rest-and-digest, immune modulation, and anti-inflammatory activity. Chronic sympathetic dominance suppresses immune function, impairs digestion, and elevates inflammatory markers.
terrain.cond: Assessment of nervous terrain: Heart Rate Variability (HRV) — low HRV = sympathetic dominance = poor vagal tone = impaired health outcomes. Orthostatic hypotension (drop >20 mmHg systolic on standing) = autonomic dysfunction, adrenal fatigue, or dehydration. Resting heart rate >80 = sympathetic excess; resting heart rate 45 to 65 = good parasympathetic tone (if athlete or conditioned).
terrain.inv: HRV (heart rate variability) — Ithlete, Elite HRV, or Polar H10 device. Orthostatic BP (supine then standing). Resting heart rate trend. Vagal nerve assessment: gag reflex, cough reflex, pupillary light reflex. Nerve conduction studies if peripheral neuropathy suspected.

therapeuticorder.sig: The foundational naturopathic principle: before adding any treatment, first remove what is blocking healing. Jared Zeff and Pamela Snider's Therapeutic Order (1987) formalises the hierarchy of naturopathic intervention. Level 1 is always addressed first.
therapeuticorder.cond: Obstacles to cure: poor nutrition, dehydration, inadequate sleep, physical inactivity, toxic exposures (food additives, environmental chemicals, medications), unhealthy relationships, smoking and alcohol, unresolved psychological trauma, digestive dysfunction (constipation, SIBO).
therapeuticorder.inv: Assessment tools: dietary diary analysis, sleep log, toxin exposure questionnaire, adverse childhood events (ACE) score, bowel assessment, Bristol Stool Chart

therapeuticorder.sign: Therapeutic Order — Level 2: Stimulate Vital Force
therapeuticorder.sig: After removing obstacles, stimulate the body's innate self-healing capacity. This level uses modalities that activate the Vis Medicatrix Naturae without adding specific therapeutic agents.
therapeuticorder.cond: Key modalities at Level 2: Therapeutic fasting (activates autophagy, immune reset, metabolic repair). Hydrotherapy (stimulates circulation, immune response, lymphatic drainage). Therapeutic exercise (mitochondrial biogenesis, anti-inflammatory myokines). Mind-body practices — yoga, pranayama, meditation (vagal tone, HPA axis reset). Adequate sunlight exposure (circadian entrainment, vitamin D synthesis).
therapeuticorder.inv: Measure change: energy levels, sleep quality (PSQI), mood (PHQ-9), bowel function, inflammatory markers (CRP, ESR), cortisol diurnal curve before and after intervention period (minimum 6 weeks)

therapeuticorder.sign: Therapeutic Order — Level 3: Strengthen Weakened Systems
therapeuticorder.sig: Identify and therapeutically support specific physiological systems that are functioning below optimal. This includes the digestive, immune, endocrine, cardiovascular, and nervous systems — using specific nutritional and herbal support.
therapeuticorder.cond: Digestive system: digestive enzymes (bromelain, papain), probiotics, prebiotics, glutamine (gut lining integrity), zinc carnosine (gastric mucosal protection). Immune system: vitamin C, vitamin D, zinc, elderberry (Sambucus nigra), Astragalus membranaceus, beta-glucans. Endocrine: adaptogenic herbs (Ashwagandha, Rhodiola, Eleuthero), thyroid-supporting nutrients (selenium, iodine, zinc), blood sugar support (berberine, chromium, magnesium).
therapeuticorder.inv: Functional markers to track by system: Gut — secretory IgA, faecal calprotectin, stool PCR. Immune — NK cell activity, lymphocyte subtypes. Endocrine — full thyroid panel, diurnal cortisol, sex hormones. Cardiovascular — lipid panel, CRP, homocysteine, fibrinogen.

therapeuticorder.sign: Therapeutic Order — Level 4: Correct Structural Integrity
therapeuticorder.sig: Address physical or structural factors impeding health — musculoskeletal alignment, fascial restriction, postural dysfunction, and lymphatic obstruction. In naturopathic medicine, structure and function are inseparable.
therapeuticorder.cond: Naturopathic structural modalities: Spinal manipulation (osteopathic or chiropractic principles). Massage therapy (Swedish, deep tissue, lymphatic drainage). Yoga asana (postural correction, fascial lengthening, joint mobilisation). Visceral manipulation (abdominal organ mobility). Craniosacral therapy. Hydrotherapy (thermal effects on fascia and circulation).
therapeuticorder.inv: Postural assessment (plumb line analysis), gait analysis, leg length discrepancy, spinal range of motion, hip-to-waist ratio, lymphoedema assessment, orthopantomogram (dental and jaw structural assessment if indicated)

therapeuticorder.sign: Therapeutic Order — Level 5: Address Pathology with Natural Agents
therapeuticorder.sig: Use specific natural therapeutic agents to address established pathological processes. These agents should be chosen based on evidence, safety profile, and the individual's constitution and terrain.
therapeuticorder.cond: Examples: Curcumin (NF-kB inhibition, anti-inflammatory). Omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA — eicosanoid balance, membrane integrity). Berberine (blood sugar, lipid regulation, gut microbiome modulation). Melatonin (circadian regulation, antioxidant, anti-inflammatory). Coenzyme Q10 (mitochondrial support, especially if on statins). Vitamin D (immune modulation, anti-inflammatory). Magnesium glycinate (muscle, nerve, sleep, blood pressure).
therapeuticorder.inv: Track evidence-based biomarkers matched to each intervention: Curcumin — CRP, ESR. Omega-3 — omega-3 index (target >8%). Berberine — HbA1c, fasting glucose, HOMA-IR. CoQ10 — if on statin therapy (statin-induced myopathy risk). Vitamin D — 25-OH vitamin D (target 75 to 150 nmol per litre).

therapeuticorder.sign: Therapeutic Order — Level 6: Address Pathology with Pharmacological Agents
therapeuticorder.sig: When natural agents at Level 5 are insufficient, pharmaceutical agents may be required. The naturopathic physician acknowledges this need without prejudice — the therapeutic order ensures that pharmaceuticals are only reached after lower levels have been genuinely and adequately explored.
therapeuticorder.cond: Indications for pharmacological intervention: life-threatening conditions requiring immediate stabilisation, failure of Levels 1 to 5 after adequate therapeutic trial (minimum 3 to 6 months), conditions with irreversible pathology (type 1 diabetes mellitus, hypothyroidism with absent thyroid tissue, Addison's disease), acute infection with systemic involvement.
therapeuticorder.inv: When prescribing at Level 6: continue all applicable lower-level interventions (nutrition, lifestyle, supplements) alongside pharmacotherapy. Monitor for nutrient depletions caused by medications (e.g., statins deplete CoQ10, metformin depletes B12, proton pump inhibitors deplete magnesium and B12 and iron).

therapeuticorder.sig: Surgery is the highest level of intervention — reserved for conditions that cannot be adequately managed with other means. Naturopathic medicine does not reject surgery; it ensures that surgery is used when truly necessary and that peri-operative naturopathic care optimises outcomes and recovery.
therapeuticorder.cond: Peri-operative naturopathic support: Pre-operative — optimise nutritional status (protein, zinc, vitamin C, vitamin D, vitamin A for wound healing), reduce inflammatory load, ensure adequate haemoglobin. Post-operative — support wound healing (zinc, bromelain, vitamin C), prevent constipation (magnesium, fibre, hydration), address post-anaesthetic liver support (milk thistle, NAC), lymphatic drainage massage, scar tissue management (castor oil packs).
therapeuticorder.inv: Pre-operative bloods: CBC (haemoglobin, platelets), albumin, HbA1c (if diabetic), electrolytes, coagulation studies. Post-operative monitoring: wound healing score, bowel function restoration timeline, energy recovery trajectory, nutritional markers at 6 and 12 weeks.

healingcrisis.sig: A healing reaction (also called healing crisis or Herxheimer reaction) occurs when the body, stimulated by treatment, begins eliminating accumulated toxins or resolving chronic suppressed pathology faster than normal elimination can process. It is a positive prognostic sign when properly recognised.
healingcrisis.cond: Classic healing reaction features: (1) Onset within 1 to 5 days of commencing treatment. (2) Duration typically 24 to 72 hours, rarely up to 7 days. (3) Symptoms are exaggerated forms of existing complaints — not new pathology. (4) Patient reports a paradoxical sense of improvement beneath the acute worsening. (5) Increased eliminative activity (sweating, loose stools, increased urination, increased nasal discharge, skin eruption). (6) Low-grade fever may be present (below 38.5 degrees Celsius).
healingcrisis.inv: Management: reduce treatment intensity by 50%, increase water intake (minimum 2.5 litres per day), support elimination (warm baths, enemas if needed, diaphoretic herbs such as elder flower), rest, reassure patient that this is a positive sign. If worsening persists beyond 72 hours or patient feels globally worse — reassess as disease crisis.

healingcrisis.sig: A specific type of healing reaction triggered by rapid microbial death — originally described in syphilis treatment but now recognised in any antimicrobial intervention including SIBO treatment, candida treatment, Lyme disease, and aggressive probiotic introduction.
healingcrisis.cond: Mechanism: dying microorganisms release endotoxins (lipopolysaccharide from gram-negative bacteria cell walls), triggering systemic inflammatory response. Symptoms: flu-like symptoms (malaise, myalgia, low-grade fever, headache), worsening of original symptoms, GI disturbance. Timeline: onset within hours to 2 days of antimicrobial intervention, resolves within 3 to 7 days.
healingcrisis.inv: Management: reduce antimicrobial dose by 50% temporarily, increase water intake, support hepatic detoxification (milk thistle, NAC, B-vitamins), activated charcoal (500 mg twice daily, away from medications) to bind endotoxins, liposomal vitamin C (2 to 4 g per day), electrolyte replacement.

healingcrisis.sig: The skin is the largest eliminative organ. During detoxification or healing, rashes, increased body odour, boils, pimples, and eczema flares may appear. In naturopathic Hering's Law framework, skin eruption during internal healing is considered a positive centrifugal (outward) movement.
healingcrisis.cond: Types of skin healing reactions: (1) Miliaria (heat rash) — from increased sweating during diaphoretic treatment. (2) Eczema flare — during gut healing (gut-skin axis activation). (3) Pimples and boils — increased sebaceous gland elimination. (4) Rosacea flare — during SIBO or gut dysbiosis treatment. Key principle: do not suppress skin eruptions during detoxification — support elimination instead.
healingcrisis.inv: Management: increase sweating (sauna therapy, exercise, diaphoretic herbal teas — elder flower, ginger, yarrow), dry skin brushing (lymphatic stimulation), cool-water rinse after sweating, clay packs over eruption sites (drawing action), calendula cream (topical anti-inflammatory). Distinguish from genuine allergic reaction (urticaria with whealing, systemic symptoms — treat conventionally).

healingcrisis.sig: Gastrointestinal elimination reactions during detoxification: loose stools, increased flatulence, nausea, and temporary changes in bowel frequency are common and expected. These reflect the liver dumping bile-bound toxins into the gut and the gut microbiome rapidly shifting during probiotic or dietary intervention.
healingcrisis.cond: Distinction from true adverse reaction: expected GI healing reactions are transient (2 to 5 days), not associated with dehydration or weight loss, improve on reducing treatment dose, accompanied by sense of improvement in other areas. Red flags requiring reassessment: bloody stools, dehydration, fever above 39 degrees Celsius, symptoms persisting beyond 7 days, severe abdominal pain.
healingcrisis.inv: Management: reduce fibre or probiotic dose temporarily, activated charcoal (500 mg, twice daily, separate from supplements), ginger tea (anti-nausea, prokinetic), psyllium husk (stool bulking), oral rehydration if diarrhoea exceeds 4 stools per day, reassess diagnosis if symptoms persist.

healingcrisis.sig: Body-stored emotional trauma can be released during somatic healing, fasting, deep massage, or yoga practice. Emotional healing reactions include: unexpected crying, irritability, vivid or disturbing dreams, emotional lability, temporary anxiety or low mood, memory surfacing of old events.
healingcrisis.cond: Context for understanding: Peter Levine's somatic experiencing model, Wilhelm Reich's character armour, and Bessel van der Kolk's trauma-body research all support that the body stores unresolved emotional experiences as somatic patterns. Physical treatment can release these. This is generally a positive prognostic sign.
healingcrisis.inv: Management: do not dismiss as psychological instability. Validate the experience. Suggest journaling, somatic or trauma-informed therapy referral, reduce treatment intensity temporarily, ensure adequate social support. Refer to psychologist or trauma-trained therapist if reactions are intense or prolonged (>1 week). Discontinue treatment if patient is destabilised.

healingcrisis.sign: Monitoring and Dose Titration — Avoiding Overwhelm
healingcrisis.sig: The key clinical skill in naturopathic practice is titrating treatment intensity to match the patient's vital force strength and detoxification capacity. Too little = no therapeutic effect. Too much = healing crisis overwhelming the patient. Optimal dose = gradual progressive improvement without overwhelming reactions.
healingcrisis.cond: Titration principle (start low, go slow): introduce one change at a time, especially in sensitive patients (elderly, children, chronic fatigue, autoimmune conditions, chemical sensitivities). Wait 7 to 14 days between new introductions to accurately attribute any reactions. Begin with elimination (removing obstacles) before adding therapeutic agents.
healingcrisis.inv: Monitoring schedule: week 1 to 2 — establish baseline metrics (energy 0 to 10, sleep quality, bowel function, main symptom severity). Review at each consultation. Track using validated scales: PSQI (sleep), VAS (pain), PHQ-9 (mood), Functional Medicine Timeline and Matrix. Adjust dose based on response trajectory.
